cards.q: What is the topic of Chapter 5?
cards.a: Tablespaces and Data Files (الصفحة 2)

cards.q: According to the Table of Contents, what fundamental concept is covered in Chapter 5?
cards.a: What is a tablespace? (الصفحة 3)

cards.q: Which topic addresses how table data is stored?
cards.a: How Table Data Is Stored (الصفحة 3)

cards.q: What overall subject does this chapter belong to?
cards.a: Oracle Database Administration (الصفحة 2)

cards.q: What is a tablespace?
cards.a: A logical container that allows you to manage groups of data files, the physical files on disk that consume space. (الصفحة 4)

cards.q: What entity represents any database object that stores data and requires space in a tablespace?
cards.a: Segment (الصفحة 4)

cards.q: What is an extent, and how is storage allocated using it?
cards.a: An extent is a set of consecutively numbered Oracle blocks within one datafile. Storage is allocated by extent. (الصفحة 4)

cards.q: How is a datafile physically composed?
cards.a: A datafile is physically made up of a number of operating system blocks. (الصفحة 4)

cards.q: What is a tablespace?
cards.a: A logical container that allows you to manage groups of data files, the physical files on disk that consume space. (الصفحة 8)

cards.q: How do data files persist compared to tablespaces?
cards.a: Data files persist whether the database is open or closed, while tablespaces only exist while the database is up and running. (الصفحة 8)

cards.q: Through what means can a tablespace be viewed and managed?
cards.a: It is visible through data dictionary views (such as DBA_TABLESPACES); you manage them through SQL*Plus or graphical tools. (الصفحة 8)

cards.q: What are the characteristics of data files?
cards.a: They have a physical presence, can be viewed outside the database through OS utilities (such as ls and dir), and consume space on disk. (الصفحة 8)

cards.q: What are tablespaces in a database?
cards.a: Logical storage units into which a database is divided; the DBA uses them to control disk space allocation, assign specific space quotas for database users, and manage data availability. (الصفحة 10)

cards.q: What does the segment entity represent in a database?
cards.a: Any database object that stores data and therefore requires space in a tablespace; examples include tables, index segments, and undo segments. (الصفحة 10)

cards.q: What is an extent?
cards.a: A set of consecutively numbered Oracle blocks within one datafile, which is how storage is allocated. (الصفحة 10)

cards.q: What is the smallest unit of data used by a database?
cards.a: The data block, whose size is controlled by the parameter DB_BLOCK_SIZE and can never be changed after database creation. (الصفحة 12)

cards.q: When a table is created, what object is created specifically to hold its data?
cards.a: A segment (الصفحة 14)

cards.q: What term describes the unit in which a row is ultimately stored within a database block?
cards.a: A row piece (الصفحة 14)

cards.q: Name two circumstances that can cause an entire row to be stored across multiple pieces.
cards.a: When an inserted row is too large to fit into a single block (chained row), or when an update causes an existing row to outgrow the available free space of the current block (migrated row) (الصفحة 14)

cards.q: What type of chaining occurs when row pieces are in the same block, often used when a table has more than 255 columns?
cards.a: Intrablock chaining (الصفحة 14)

cards.q: What does the SYSTEM tablespace provide storage for?
cards.a: The Oracle data dictionary objects; it stores all objects owned by the SYS user. (Page 18)

cards.q: What is the SYSAUX tablespace used as?
cards.a: An auxiliary tablespace used as a data repository for Oracle database tools, such as Enterprise Manager, Statspack, LogMiner, and Logical Standby. (Page 18)

cards.q: What information does the UNDO tablespace store?
cards.a: Information required to undo the effects of a transaction (insert, update, delete, or merge), especially if a transaction is purposely rolled back. (Page 18)

cards.q: When creating a database, what are the five typically created tablespaces?
cards.a: SYSTEM, SYSAUX, UNDO, TEMP, USERS. (Page 16)

cards.q: What are TEMP tablespaces typically used for?
cards.a: They are typically used for sorting query results or providing temporary storage when creating or rebuilding indexes. (الصفحة 20)

cards.q: Can permanent database objects be stored in a TEMP tablespace?
cards.a: No, the space is only used for transient data and cannot contain permanent database objects. (الصفحة 20)

cards.q: What is the USERS tablespace often used as?
cards.a: It is often used as a default permanent tablespace for table and index data for users. (الصفحة 20)

cards.q: If no tablespace is specified during object creation, where will the object be created by default?
cards.a: By default, the object is created in the default permanent tablespace. (الصفحة 20)

cards.q: Historically, why did DBAs separate table and index data into different tablespaces?
cards.a: To reduce input/output (I/O) contention because the data files could be placed on different disks with separate controllers. (الصفحة 22)

cards.q: Besides performance reasons, what are valid reasons for creating multiple tablespaces for table and index data?
cards.a: Backup and recovery requirements may be different; indexes may have storage requirements different from the table data; or to simplify management by logically grouping tables and indexes separately. (الصفحة 22)

cards.q: When might a user want to separate a LOB column into its own tablespace?
cards.a: Because they want to manage the LOB tablespace storage characteristics differently from those of the regular table data. (الصفحة 22)

cards.q: How does grouping objects in separate tablespaces assist with space management, even when using Automatic Segment Space Management (ASSM)?
cards.a: The grouping assists in the management of the objects as well as the automated space management. (الصفحة 22)

cards.q: When creating a tablespace in Toad for Oracle, what three items must be determined on the "Basic Info" page?
cards.a: A name for the new tablespace, its type (Permanent, Temporary, Undo), and the Extent Management setting (الصفحة 26)

cards.q: What is the recommended choice for Extent Management when creating a tablespace?
cards.a: Allocated Managed (الصفحة 26)

cards.q: When determining allocation size and segment management for a new tablespace, what options are available?
cards.a: Auto Allocate Extent Sizes, Uniform Extent Sizes, and Auto Segment Space Management (الصفحة 26)

cards.q: What are the possible types that determine a new tablespace's content type?
cards.a: Permanent, Temporary, or Undo (الصفحة 26)

cards.q: When creating a datafile, what information must be entered along with the Datafile name and full path?
cards.a: The initial datafile size, whether it is Auto Extent (requiring determination of additional space), and if it has a maximum size limit. (الصفحة 28)

cards.q: What are the two ways extents can be allocated in a tablespace?
cards.a: Automatic ('autoallocate'), where system manages extent sizes; or Uniform, which specifies management with uniform extents of a specified size (default is 1 MB). (الصفحة 30)

cards.q: What are the key characteristics of an Undo Tablespace?
cards.a: It is used to store undo segments, cannot contain any other objects, and can only use the DATAFILE and EXTENT MANAGEMENT clauses. (الصفحة 30)

cards.q: Which type of tablespace cannot be specified with 'Automatic' extent allocation?
cards.a: A temporary tablespace. (الصفحة 30)

cards.q: What is an advantage of using the bigfile feature when creating a tablespace?
cards.a: It has the potential to create very large files (e.g., up to 128TB with a 32KB block size). (الصفحة 32)

cards.q: What is a potential disadvantage of using a bigfile tablespace?
cards.a: If space runs out on the file system that supports the data file, you cannot expand the size of the tablespace. (الصفحة 32)

cards.q: Who automatically owns the undo segments in an instance?
cards.a: SYS. (الصفحة 32)

cards.q: What clause must be used to create a bigfile tablespace?
cards.a: Use the BIGFILE clause. (الصفحة 32)

cards.q: Temporary tablespaces are primarily used for what type of operation?
cards.a: Sort operations (الصفحة 34)

cards.q: What permanent objects cannot reside in a temporary tablespace?
cards.a: No permanent schema objects can reside in a temporary tablespace. (الصفحة 34)

cards.q: If a database is created without a default temporary tablespace, what tablespace is assigned by default to users created without a TEMPORARY TABLESPACE clause?
cards.a: The SYSTEM tablespace (الصفحة 34)

cards.q: What command sequence is used to set a default temporary tablespace for an existing database?
cards.a: Create the temporary tablespace, then alter the database using ALTER DATABASE DEFAULT TEMPORARY TABLESPACE temp (الصفحة 34)
